,

Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).
In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.
And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:
If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:
Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
,In Smalltalk, you can access class variables (also known as instance variables) using the dot notation or by sending a message to the object.
In your case, you have an instance of `Class1` stored in the `start-time` variable of `Class2`. To access the `year` variable of that `Class1` instance, you can use the dot notation like this:
This sends the `year:` message to the `start-time` object, which is an instance of `Class1`, with the argument `2012`. This will set the `year` variable of that object to `2012`.
Alternatively, you can use the syntax `start-time year := 2012`, which is equivalent to the previous example.
Here's a breakdown of what's happening:
* `start-time` is an instance of `Class1`, which has a `year` variable.
* `year:` is a message sent to the `start-time` object, which sets the value of the `year` variable.
* `2012` is the argument passed to the `year:` message, which sets the value of the `year` variable to `2012`.
Note that in Smalltalk, variables are not accessed directly using a dot notation like `start-time.year`. Instead, you send a message to the object to access or modify its variables.

You can also use this syntax to access other variables of the `Class1` instance, such as `start-time month` or `start-time day`, and so on.",206,,,,,0.1,0.4933238966538168,1,"['Instead , you just send messages to objects .']","['Instead , you send a message to the object to access or modify its variables .']",176,1420,322,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

GenAI,Pharo method with multiple arguments,"

I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error ""Variable or expression expected..""
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :
<pre><code>    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,The issue lies in the method declaration syntax. In Pharo, when you declare a method with multiple arguments, you need to separate the keywords with spaces, not colons.
Here's the correct method declaration syntax:
MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2: argValue3
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue3
Or, if you want to make it more readable:
MethodName: arg1 aStringValue arg2 anObject arg3 aNumber
^ self var1: aStringValue var2: anObject var3: aNumber
In this example, `arg1`, `arg2`, and `arg3` are the keywords, and `aStringValue`, `anObject`, and `aNumber` are the argument names.
When you call the method, you would do it like this:

This would pass the string `'hello'` as the first argument, the current object (`self`) as the second argument, and the number `42` as the third argument.",134,,,,,0.0,0.5482609968784583,0,[],[],175,1414,252,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

,

The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.
Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.
Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:
With these three methods you should be able to run your example.
Some additional comments on the coding style:
Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the ""constructor"" method (in Smalltalk we call these ""instance creation"" methods). For instance,
Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.
Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in
which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.

In Pharo (or Smalltalk in general), constructors are not implemented as separate methods, but rather as a part of the class initialization process. The issue you're facing is due to a misunderstanding of how to define and use constructors in Smalltalk.
Here's what's wrong with your approach:
1. **Method naming**: In Smalltalk, the constructor method is typically named `new` (not `newName:newAge:`). The `new` method is a special method that returns a new instance of the class.

2. **Method signature**: The `new` method should have no arguments (or a variable number of arguments, but that's advanced). In your case, you defined a method with two arguments, `aName` and `aAge`, which is not the standard way to define a constructor.
To fix the issue, follow these steps:

In a class definition, you need to define a class-side method (a method on the class, not on instances) named `new`. This method will return a new instance of the class, initialized with the desired values.
Person class >> new: aName age: anAge
Now, in the Playground, you can create a new `Person` instance using the `new` method:
This will create a new `Person` instance with `name` set to `'Carl'` and `age` set to `10`.

Remember, in Smalltalk, the `new` method is a special method that returns a new instance of the class. It's not a regular method that you can call on an existing instance.",276,,,,,0.0,0.5445335001386341,0,[],[],222,1375,395,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct
GenAI,SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:
When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.
Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

,The issue lies in the fact that `Transcript` is a class in Smalltalk, not an instance. `Transcript` is used to represent the system's transcript, which is a stream that displays the output of the system. You cannot send the `show:` message to the `Transcript` class, as it's not an instance that can receive messages.

To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` object using the `Transcript`, you need to get an instance of the `Transcript` class, which is typically done using `Transcript show:`. However, the correct syntax is to send the `show:` message to the `Transcript` instance, passing the object you want to print as an argument.
This code will output the contents of the `OrderedCollection` instance `oc` to the system's transcript.
When you write `self Transcript show: self

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows
Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
<li>It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
• when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:
yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.

,The concept you're struggling with is called ""subclass responsibility"" (not subclassResponsibility, which is a method), and it's a common pattern in object-oriented programming, particularly in Smalltalk-based languages like Pharo.

Subclass responsibility is a design pattern that ensures subclasses provide a specific implementation for a method that is declared in their superclass. In other words, a superclass delegates the responsibility of implementing a method to its subclasses.
**Why do you need to use subclass responsibility?**

In your example, `AbstractDictionary` is an abstract class that provides a common interface for all dictionaries. The `size` method is part of this interface, but its implementation varies depending on the specific dictionary subclass.

By declaring `size` in `AbstractDictionary` with a `subclassResponsibility` implementation, you're saying: ""Hey, subclasses, you need to provide your own implementation for `size`, because it's specific to each of you.""
1. **Polymorphism**: By declaring `size` in the abstract superclass, you ensure that all subclasses respond to this message, even if they have different implementations.
2. **Flexibility**: Subclasses can provide their own implementation for `size`, which allows for different dictionary types to have different size calculation strategies.
3. **Code reuse**: You can write code that works with any dictionary subclass, without knowing the specific implementation details of each subclass.

If you implemented `size` in `AbstractDictionary` with `^self size`, it would create an infinite recursion. When `size` is called on an instance of `AbstractDictionary`, it would call itself, and so on. This is because `self` refers to the current object, which is an instance of `AbstractDictionary`, not one of its subclasses.
Since `AbstractArrayDictionary` is an abstract class, it should not provide a concrete implementation for `size`. Instead, it should either:
1. Inherit the `size` method from `AbstractDictionary`, which would delegate the responsibility to its subclasses.
2. Declare its own `size` method with a `subclassResponsibility` implementation, if it needs to provide additional guidance to its subclasses.
In your case, since `AbstractArrayDictionary` has a specific implementation for `size`, it's better to remove the method from this class and let its subclasses provide their own implementation.
* Use subclass responsibility to delegate the implementation of a method to subclasses, ensuring polymorphism and flexibility.
* Avoid implementing methods in abstract superclasses with recursive calls to `self`.
* Remove the `size` method from `AbstractArrayDictionary` and let its subclasses provide their own implementation.",388,,,,,0.0,0.5382850775019518,0,[],[],601,990,577,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,I'd be happy to help you with your Pharo 4.0 syntax questions.
**1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error?**

The issue lies in the way you're defining and calling the class method. In Pharo, class methods (also known as static methods) are defined on the class side, and instance methods are defined on the instance side.
Your class method definition is correct:
However, when calling this class method, you should use the following syntax:
CreateUser createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
Note the absence of the `new` keyword, which is used to create a new instance of a class. Since `createNewUser:` is a class method, you call it directly on the class `CreateUser`.
**2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java?**

While the concept is similar, there are some differences between Pharo and Java. In Pharo, class variables (also known as class instance variables) are stored in the class object, whereas instance variables are stored in individual instances of the class.

In Java, static variables and methods are essentially global variables and functions that belong to the class, not instances of the class. In Pharo, class variables and methods are more like instance variables and methods of the class object itself.
* Pharo: Class variables (class instance variables) are stored in the class object, and class methods (static methods) operate on the class object.
* Java: Static variables and methods are essentially global variables and functions that belong to the class, not instances of the class.
**3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?**
Yes, that's correct! In Pharo, you can add accessor methods (getters and setters) to your class to access instance variables. These accessor methods can be defined on the instance side of the class.

GenAI,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
If your Pharo image enters an infinite loop, you can break out of it using the following steps:

1. **Press `Alt + .`** (Windows/Linux) or **`Cmd + .`** (Mac) to activate the **Process Browser**. This will pause the execution of your image and open a new window displaying the current process list.

2. **Identify the problematic process**: In the Process Browser, look for the process that is consuming high CPU or is stuck in an infinite loop. You can sort the processes by CPU usage or name to help you identify the culprit.
3. **Select the problematic process**: Click on the process to select it.

4. **Terminate the process**: Right-click on the selected process and choose **`Terminate`** (or use the shortcut **`Ctrl + Shift + T`**). This will kill the infinite loop and release the resources associated with it.

Alternatively, you can also use the **`Ctrl + Shift + C`** shortcut to **terminate the current process** without opening the Process Browser. This will interrupt the execution of the current process and return you to a responsive image.

To avoid losing work in the future, consider using Pharo's built-in **`File Out`** feature, which allows you to save your image periodically. You can also use external tools like **`File History`** or **`Iceberg`** to maintain a version history of your code.

Remember to be cautious when working with infinite loops, as they can cause system instability or crashes. Always test your code in a controlled environment and use debugging tools to identify and fix issues before they cause problems.",254,,,,,0.0,0.5381699322883896,0,[],[],178,1431,342,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

**First question: Why does the `!` symbol still appear in the left part of my classes after Accepting the Changes?**

The `!` symbol indicates that the class definition is not yet compiled or saved to the image. When you make changes to a class definition, Pharo marks it as ""dirty"" with the `!` symbol until you explicitly save the changes. To get rid of the `!` symbol, you need to save the changes by clicking on the ""Save"" button or pressing `Ctrl+S` (or `Cmd+S` on a Mac). This will compile and save the class definition to the image.
**Second question: Did I make the right decision to put those methods in the class side?**

Yes, you made the right decision. In Pharo Smalltalk, the class side is used for class methods, which are methods that can be invoked without creating an instance of the class. Class methods are typically used for factory methods, singleton access, or other class-level functionality.
In your example, the `count` method is a class method because it returns a count of all instances of the class, not a specific instance. It makes sense to put this method on the class side.

GenAI,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because, although it does not have explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns, it still performs type checks at runtime. This means that the system checks the type compatibility of objects when they are used, rather than at compile-time.

In Smalltalk, objects are instances of classes, and each class defines a set of methods and variables that can be accessed by objects of that class. When an object is sent a message (i.e., a method is called on it), the system checks whether the object's class responds to that message. If it does, the method is executed
column2: if not, a runtime error occurs. This process is known as ""dynamic dispatch"" or ""late binding"".

column1: The key difference between ""dynamically typed"" and ""untyped"" is that, in a dynamically typed system, type checks are still performed, albeit at runtime rather than at compile-time. In an untyped system, no type checks are performed, and objects can be used in any way without restriction.
To illustrate the difference, consider the following example:
In a statically typed language like Java, you might declare a variable `x` to be of type `String`:
The compiler checks that `x` is assigned a value of type `String`, and prevents you from assigning a value of a different type.
In contrast, in Smalltalk, you might assign an object to a variable `x` without explicitly declaring its type:

At runtime, when you send a message to `x`, the system checks whether the object's class responds to that message. If you try to send a message that is not understood by the object's class, a runtime error occurs.

So, to answer your question, Smalltalk does not explicitly give types to variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime. Instead, it uses the object's class to determine its type, and performs type checks dynamically when messages are sent to objects.

It's worth noting that ""dynamically typed"" is not the same as ""dynamically type-checked"". Dynamically typed refers to the fact that type checks are performed at runtime, whereas dynamically type-checked refers to the process of checking the type",345,,,,,0.0833333333333333,0.583776914670659,1,"['When an object receives a message , it first looks in the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message .']","[""When an object is sent a message ( i.e. , a method is called on it ) , the system checks whether the object 's class responds to that message .""]",182,1419,468,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,In Pharo, `instanceVariableNames` and `classVariableNames` are two distinct concepts that serve different purposes.

In Pharo, an instance variable is a variable that is associated with an instance of a class. Each instance of the class has its own copy of the variable, and changes made to the variable in one instance do not affect other instances.
`instanceVariableNames` is a class method that specifies the names of the instance variables for a class. In other words, it defines the variables that will be created for each instance of the class.

GenAI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default because of the language's fundamental design principles, which emphasize simplicity, flexibility, and expressive power.

Smalltalk's creators, led by Alan Kay, aimed to design a language that would allow programmers to focus on the essence of the problem they were trying to solve, rather than getting bogged down in low-level implementation details. They drew inspiration from Lisp, Simula, and other pioneering languages, seeking to create a more intuitive, interactive, and dynamic environment.

1. **Message Passing**: In Smalltalk, objects communicate by sending and receiving messages (method calls). This approach encourages a more declarative, high-level style of programming, where objects collaborate to achieve a common goal.

2. **Everything is an Object**: In Smalltalk, every entity, including classes, methods, and blocks, is an object. This uniformity simplifies the language and enables more flexible, generic programming.
3. **Late Binding**: Methods are bound to their implementations at runtime, rather than at compile-time. This allows for greater flexibility, as methods can be modified or replaced dynamically.
By default, methods return ""self"" to maintain a consistent, intuitive, and efficient programming model. This design choice has several benefits:

1. **Method Chaining**: As you mentioned, returning ""self"" enables method chaining, where a series of messages can be sent to an object without explicit intermediate returns. This leads to more concise, expressive code.
2. **Fluent Interface**: Returning ""self"" allows for a more fluent, natural language style, where objects can be manipulated and queried in a straightforward, pipelined manner.
3. **Reduced Boilerplate Code**: By returning ""self"" implicitly, programmers can focus on the essential logic of their methods, rather than cluttering their code with explicit returns.
4. **Improved Code Readability**: When methods return ""self"" by default, the code becomes more readable, as the focus is on the actions being performed, rather than the return values.

,I see you're struggling with implementing a switch statement in Pharo Smalltalk!
In Smalltalk, there is no built-in `switch` statement like in other languages. Instead, you can use a combination of messages and blocks to achieve similar behavior.
In your case, you can use a cascade of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages, as you've already attempted. However, this can lead to deeply nested code, making it hard to read and maintain.
A better approach is to use a `Dictionary` to map command strings to their corresponding actions. This is often referred to as a ""dispatch table"" or ""command table"".
Here's an updated version of your code that demonstrates this approach:

1. We define a `commandTable` dictionary that maps command strings to their corresponding actions. Each action is a block that takes a single argument, `val`.
2. In the `do:` block, we split the command into `bo` (command string) and `val` (value string).

3. We use the `commandTable` to dispatch to the corresponding action based on the `bo` command string. If the command is not found in the table, we assert false (you can customize this behavior as needed).
4. We pass `valInt` as an argument to the dispatched action block.
This approach is more concise, easier to read, and allows you to add or remove commands and actions without modifying the underlying logic.

RAG,Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?,"

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
,As a Rubyist starting to explore Smalltalk, you're considering Squeak or Pharo as your implementation of choice. Both are popular and well-established options, but they have some key differences.

Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language inspired by Smalltalk (p. 1). It was forked from Squeak in 2008 and has since evolved into a highly portable and flexible platform, running on various operating systems, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (p. 2). Pharo's virtual machine is written entirely in a subset of Pharo itself, making it easy to simulate, debug, analyze, and change from within Pharo itself (p. 2).

One of Pharo's core principles is to reinvent the essence of Smalltalk through evolutionary and incremental changes, rather than attempting a big-bang approach (p. 2). This has resulted in a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments. Pharo serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework (p. 1 and p. 2).

In contrast, Squeak is a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80, from which Pharo was forked (p. 2). While Squeak is still an active and popular platform, Pharo has gained significant traction in recent years, with a growing community and a wide range of innovative projects, including multimedia applications, educational platforms, and commercial web development environments (p. 2).
When deciding between Squeak and Pharo, consider the following pros and cons:
* Written entirely in Pharo, making it easy to simulate, debug, analyze, and change from within Pharo itself
* Growing community and a wide range of innovative projects
* Robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments
* May have a steeper learning curve due to its unique approach to Smalltalk
* Wider range of existing resources and documentation
* Still an active and popular platform
* May be less portable and flexible than Pharo
* Less innovative and less actively developed than Pharo

Ultimately, the choice between Squeak and Pharo depends on your personal preferences and goals. If you're looking for a more modern, flexible, and innovative platform with a growing community, Pharo might be the better choice. However, if you prefer a more established and mature platform with a wider range of existing resources, Squeak could be the way to go.

If you want to use regular expressions:
,The question asks if it's possible to match all words in a phrase using a regular expression. The answer is yes, it is possible.

The regular expression `[A-Za-z]*` is not sufficient to match all words in a phrase because it only matches a single word. To match all words, you can use the regular expression `\w+` which matches one or more word characters (letters, digits, or underscores).

In Pharo, you can use the `matchesCollect:` method to collect all matches of a regular expression within a string. For example, `'Jack meet Jill' regex: '\w+' matchesCollect: [:word | word]` will return an ordered collection of all words in the string (`OrderedCollection('Jack' 'meet' 'Jill')`). (p. 339)

Alternatively, you can use the `allRegexMatches:` method to get a collection of all matches of a regular expression within a string. For example, `'Jack and Jill went up the hill' allRegexMatches: '\w+'` will return an ordered collection of all words in the string (`OrderedCollection('Jack' 'and' 'Jill' 'went' 'up' 'the' 'hill')`). (p. 340)

Note that the `matchesCollect:` and `allRegexMatches:` methods return a collection of all matches, not just the first match. This is in contrast to the `matches:` method which only returns a boolean indicating whether the whole string matches the regular expression. (p. 341)

In summary, to match all words in a phrase, you can use the regular expression `\w+` and the `matchesCollect:` or `allRegexMatches:` methods in Pharo.",228,,,,,0.2857142857142857,0.6436508283628106,2,"[""list > > > an OrderedCollection('Jack ' ' meet ' ' Jill ' ) regex : matchesCollect : evaluates a one - argument aBlock for every match of the regular expression within the receiver string ."", ""' Jack and Jill went up the hill ' allRegexMatches : ' \\w+ ' > > > an OrderedCollection('Jack ' ' and ' ' Jill ' ' went ' ' up ' ' the ' ' hill ' )""]","['In Pharo , you can use the ` matchesCollect : ` method to collect all matches of a regular expression within a string .', ""For example , ` ' Jack and Jill went up the hill ' allRegexMatches : ' \\w+ ' ` will return an ordered collection of all words in the string ( ` OrderedCollection('Jack ' ' and ' ' Jill ' ' went ' ' up ' ' the ' ' hill ' ) ` ) .""]",255,1337,351,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?,"

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.

,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"" (pp. 60, 103, 105), the difference between `self` and `yourself` in Smalltalk is that `yourself` is not a valid term in Smalltalk. The correct term is `self`, which represents the receiver of the message.

`self` is a pseudo-variable that represents the object inside which the current method is executing (p. 60). It is called the ""receiver"" because this object has received the message that caused the method to execute. `self` is not a keyword, but rather a variable that is bound to the respective receiver.

In a method, `self` represents the object that received the message, and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver (p. 103). This means that when a method is called, `self` will be bound to the object that received the message, and the method lookup will start in the class of that object.

On the other hand, `super` is also a pseudo-variable that represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs (p. 105).

In summary, `self` and `super` both represent the receiver of the message, but they differ in the method lookup. `self` starts the lookup in the class of the receiver, while `super` starts the lookup in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.
Here is an example to illustrate the difference:
In this example, `self` will be bound to the respective receiver, whether it is `WebServer` or `ReliableWebServer`, and the method lookup will start in the class of the receiver (pp. 95-96).

In conclusion, `self` is a fundamental concept in Smalltalk that represents the receiver of the message, and it is essential to understand its role in method execution and lookup.",298,,,,,0.6,0.7397024851840885,6,"['Instead of lookup starting in the class of the receiver , it starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the super send occurs .', 'Important self represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver .', 'self is a keyword that refers to the object inside which the current method is executing .', '* super also refers to the receiver of the current method , but when you send a message to super , the method - lookup changes so that it starts from the superclass of the class containing the method that uses super .', 'We call it "" the receiver "" because this object 60 4.1 Syntactic elements has received the message that caused the method to execute .', ""This means that all messages sent to self are looked up by starting in the receiver 's class .""]","['` self ` starts the lookup in the class of the receiver , while ` super ` starts the lookup in the superclass of the class of the method where the ` super ` send occurs .', 'In a method , ` self ` represents the object that received the message , and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver ( p. 103 ) .', '` self ` is a pseudo - RAG,Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.
There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

,In Pharo Smalltalk, you can initialize instance variables in several ways. One way is to use lazy initialization, which means delaying the initialization of a variable until it's actually needed (p. 6.6). This approach is useful when the variable is expensive to initialize or when users don't typically expect to call a specific method.

Another way is to use class variables, which are shared between instances and classes (p. 109). You can define class variables using the `classVariableNames:` keyword in the class definition (Listing 6-29). To initialize class variables, you can use an accessor method that initializes the variable if it hasn't been initialized before (p. 111).

In your example, you want to initialize an instance variable `aColor` with a specific value. You can do this using the `initialize` method, which is called automatically when an object is instantiated (p. 93).
Here's an example of how you can initialize `aColor`:

This approach is similar to constructors in languages like Java and C++. However, in Pharo Smalltalk, the `initialize` method is called automatically when an object is created, so you don't need to explicitly call it.
Alternatively, you can use lazy initialization to delay the initialization of `aColor` until it's actually needed. For example:
This approach is useful when `aColor` is expensive to initialize or when you want to delay its initialization until it's actually needed.

It's worth noting that Pharo Smalltalk has a specific convention for naming variables, where instance variables start with a lowercase letter, and shared variables (including class variables and global variables) start with an uppercase letter (p. 109).",278,,,,,0.3846153846153846,0.6879335547817349,5,"['The initialize method is automatically executed after an object is created in Pharo .', 'The initialize method is defined on the instance side in the usual way
column2: remember that new automatically sends initialize to the newly - created instance .', 'This can be done by introducing an accessor method which , when executed , initializes the variable if it has not yet been initialized .', 'One solution is lazy initialization ( discussed earlier in this chapter ) .', 'Class variables : variables shared between instances and classes .']","[""However , in Pharo Smalltalk , the ` initialize ` method is called automatically when an object is created , so you do n't need to explicitly call it ."", 'You can do this using the ` initialize ` method , which is called automatically when an object is instantiated ( p. 93 ) .', ""To initialize class variables , you can use an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has n't been initialized before ( p. 111 ) ."", ""One way is to use lazy initialization , which means delaying the initialization of a variable until it 's actually needed ( p. 6.6 ) ."", 'Another way is to use class variables , which are shared between instances and classes ( p. 109 ) .']",179,1409,388,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk,"

I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter
It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.
2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'
For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)
The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.
4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error
<pre><code>  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:
the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.
5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random
The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)

,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"", the issue with your code is that you're trying to access the array with indices starting from 1, but in Pharo, the first element of an array is at position 1, not 0 (p. 203).
To fix your code, you should change the loop to start from 1 and go up to 31, and use the `at:put:` method to set the values of the array. Here's the corrected code:
1 to: 31 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
This code will create an array of size 31, and then loop from 1 to 31, setting each element of the array to a random value of either 0 or 1 using the `at:put:` method.
Note that you can also use the `doWithIndex:` method, which gives access to the current index, like this:
lista doWithIndex: [ :each :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
This code uses the `doWithIndex:` method to iterate over the array, and sets each element to a random value of either 0 or 1 using the `at:put:` method.

Also, be careful not to modify literal arrays, as they are kept in compiled method literal frames and may not have the value you expect the second time you execute the code (p. 204).",239,,,,,0.4,0.646336484833497,2,"['Contrary to the C convention in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 and not 0 .', 'Literal arrays are kept in compiled method literal frames ( a space where literals appearing in a method are stored ) , therefore unless you copy the array , the second time you execute the code your literal array may not have the value you expect .']","['According to the textbook "" Pharo By Example "" , the issue with your code is that you \'re trying to access the array with indices starting from 1 , but in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 , not 0', 'as they are kept in compiled method literal frames and may not have the value you expect the second time you execute the code ( p. 204 )']",197,1400,353,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

To access the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time`, you can define a class method in `Class1` to access the `year` instance variable. For example, you can add a class method `year:` to `Class1` to set the `year` instance variable:

RAG,Pharo method with multiple arguments,"

I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error ""Variable or expression expected..""
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :
<pre><code>    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"", the correct method declaration syntax for a Pharo class method that takes 3 arguments is as follows (p. 64):
`MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3`
In your case, the correct declaration would be:
`MethodName: var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2`
This is because in Pharo, keyword messages take an arbitrary number of arguments (p. 63). When calling this method, you would use the same syntax:
`ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2`

Note that in Pharo, methods are normally defined in a browser window or in the debugger, and are developed one method at a time in the context of a given class (p. 64).",107,,,,,0.0,0.6084999852771218,0,[],[],217,1372,179,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:
When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.
Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"" (pages 226-227, 204-205), the issue lies in the fact that `Transcript` is not a stream that can receive the message `show:` with an `OrderedCollection` as an argument.
To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` object using `Transcript`, you need to use a `WriteStream` and send the `print:` message to it. Here's an example:

This code creates an `OrderedCollection` object `oc`, adds elements to it, and then creates a `WriteStream` object `stream`. The `print:` message is sent to `stream` with `oc` as an argument, which adds the textual representation of `oc` to the stream. Finally, `Transcript` is sent the `show:` message with the contents of the stream as an argument, which prints the contents of `oc` to the Transcript.

Note that `Transcript` is a system console that is often used for logging system messages (page 16). It's not a stream that can receive the `show:` message with an `OrderedCollection` as an argument. Instead, you need to use a `WriteStream` to convert the `OrderedCollection` to a string, and then send that string to `Transcript` using the `show:` message.",202,,,,,0.1111111111111111,0.6125877970175209,1,['226 11.3 Streaming over collections | stream | stream : = String new writeStream .'],['| stream |   stream : = String new writeStream .'],216,1382,329,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?,"

I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

,

You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own ""if"" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.
E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"" (pages 66, 314, 195-196), in Pharo, we write `if` `elseif` `elseif` using the following syntax:
`condition ifTrue: [block to execute if true] ifFalse: [block to execute if false]`
Here, `condition` is a boolean expression, and `[block to execute if true]` and `[block to execute if false]` are blocks of code to be executed depending on the condition.
To write an `if elseif elseif` construct, you can chain multiple `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` messages together. For example:

This code checks if `x` is less than 25, and if so, executes the block `[DoSomething]`. If not, it checks if `x` is greater than 25, and if so, executes the block `[DoSomethingElse]`. If not, it executes the block `[DoAnotherThing]`.

Note that Pharo does not have a special syntax for control constructs like `if` and `loop`. Instead, these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments (pages 66, 314).",185,,,,,0.2,0.6731281001545975,1,"['Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers and collections , with blocks as arguments .']","['Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers , and collections with blocks as arguments ( pages 66 , 314 ) .']",200,1396,296,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows
Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.
My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:
And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
<li>It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
• when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to your question:

In Pharo, `subclassResponsibility` is a convention used to signal that a subclass should have implemented a particular method (p. 97, 113). It's a way to announce that the subclass should have overridden a specific method. When a method sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it means that the method is abstract and should be implemented by its subclasses (p. 311).

When a method sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it raises an error indicating that the subclass should have overridden the method (p. 187). This error message is provided by asking `thisContext` for the sender, which allows Pharo to provide a useful error message indicating which abstract method has been invoked (p. 311).

Implementing `size` as `^self size` would not work as expected because it would simply recurse indefinitely. By convention, methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered abstract, and should never be executed (p. 97). If you implement `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `^self size`, it would call itself recursively, leading to an infinite loop.

Implementing `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` and not in `AbstractDictionary` is a design choice. Since `AbstractArrayDictionary` is a more specific subclass of `AbstractDictionary`, it's reasonable to implement `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` if its subclasses (e.g., `SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) need to provide their own implementation of `size`. By implementing `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary`, you ensure that its subclasses will provide their own implementation of `size`.

If you implement `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `self subclassResponsibility`, then you should remove the implementation of `size` from `AbstractArrayDictionary`. This is because `AbstractArrayDictionary` is a subclass of `AbstractDictionary`, and it should not provide its own implementation of `size` if the abstract method is already implemented in `AbstractDictionary`.

In summary, `subclassResponsibility` is a convention used to signal that a subclass should have implemented a particular method. It's used to provide a useful error message indicating which abstract method has been invoked. Implementing `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `self subclassResponsibility` is a way to announce that subclasses should implement their own `size` method.",348,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.6045279009520137,2,"['* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .']","['When a method sends ` self subclassResponsibility ` , it means that the method is abstract and should be implemented by its subclasses ( p. 311 ) .', 'By convention , methods that send ` self subclassResponsibility ` are considered abstract , and should never be executed ( p. 97 ) .']",643,948,554,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,I'll address each of your queries and provide explanations with references to the Pharo By Example textbook.
**1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error?**

The error `MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:` indicates that Pharo doesn't understand the message `name:email:phone:` sent to the `CreateUser` class. This is because the syntax for defining a class method in Pharo is different from what you've written.

To fix this error, you should define a class method with the correct syntax. For example, if you want to create a class method `createNewUser:` that takes three arguments `name:`, `email:`, and `phone:`, you would define it as follows:
CreateUser class >> createNewUser: name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
**2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java?**

No, the concept is not the same as Java. In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, which means that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods, unlike static methods in Java (p. 92).
**3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?**

Yes, that's correct. In Pharo, instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). However, if you want to access instance variables from outside the class, you need to define accessor methods. For example, if you have an instance variable `name` in the `CreateUser` class, you can define an accessor method `name` that returns the value of the instance variable:
Then, you can call this method on an instance of the `CreateUser` class to access the `name` instance variable.
* In Pharo, comments are enclosed in double quotes `""` (p. 61).
* Local variable definitions use vertical bars `| |` (p. 61).
* Blocks are defined using square brackets `[]` (p. 61).
* Class instance variables are private to each class and are inherited by subclasses, but each subclass has its own private copies of those variables (p. 92).

RAG,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of the loop by typing `CMD-.` (that's a full stop or a period, depending on where you learned English) to interrupt the computation and open a debugger on the execution (p. 140). Alternatively, you can also edit the suspect code to insert `Halt now` and run the method, which will bring up the pre-debugger, from where you can either proceed or go into the debugger (p. 140).
Once you are in the debugger, you can examine the execution and find the problematic code. You can then fix the code and continue or quit the debugger.

Additionally, if you want to conditionally halt only in a specific context, you can use `Object>>haltIf:` method, which allows you to set a breakpoint that only triggers in a specific context (pp. 311-312).

It's also important to note that when you quit Pharo, you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects, so you can recover your work even if you lost it due to an infinite loop (p. 15).

In your case, since you lost all your work for the past two hours, you can try to recover it by restarting the image and saving it again. Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image, and if you do save your image and reopen it, you will see that things are exactly as you left them (p. 15).",240,,,,,0.7777777777777778,0.7476558333265986,7,"['If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'You can also just edit the suspect code to insert Halt now .', 'Often , you can fix the code directly in the debugger window and click Proceed to continue running the application .', 'In any case , Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image .', '* Intelligent breakpoints can be set using haltIf : , taking a method selector as its argument .', ""( that 's a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) ."", 'When you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects .']","['If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop , you can break out of the loop by typing ` CMD- . `', 'Alternatively , you can also edit the suspect code to insert ` Halt now ` and run the method , which will bring up the pre - debugger , from where you can either proceed or go into the debugger ( p. 140 ) .', 'You can then fix the code and continue or quit the debugger .', 'Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image , and if you do save your image and reopen it , you will see that things are exactly as you left them ( p. 15 ) .', 'Additionally , if you want to conditionally halt only in a specific context , you can use ` Object>>haltIf : ` method , which allows you to set a breakpoint that only triggers in a specific context ( pp . 311 - 312 ) .', ""( that 's a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) to interrupt the computation and open a debugger on the execution ( p. 140 ) ."", ""It 's also important to note that when you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects , so you can recover your work even if you lost it due to an infinite loop ( p. 15 ) .""]",220,1389,305,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

column1: In your example, you correctly put the instance variable `count` on the instance side. You also put the class methods `new` and `count` on the class side, which is correct (p. 93).

Class methods can be quite useful (p. 92). They are defined on a class and can be used to perform utility functions or instance creation methods. In your example, the `new` method increments the `count` variable when a new instance is created, and the `count` method returns the current count value (p. 93).

When initializing instance variables, it's a good practice to use lazy initialization, which means initializing the variable only when it's actually needed (p. 64). In your example, you used lazy initialization by initializing the `count` variable only when a new instance is created.

,To generate getter/setter code automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can create a method that takes a list of instance variables as input and generates the accessor methods dynamically. This approach is known as ""Dynamic Accessors"" (p. 315).
Here's an example of how you can create a method `addVariablesAndAccessors` that takes a class name and a list of instance variables as input:
class compile: variable, String cr, ' ^ ', variable.
class compile: variable, ':', String cr, ' ^ ', variable ].

This method compiles the getter and setter methods for each instance variable in the list. The getter method has the same name as the instance variable, and the setter method has the same name with a trailing `:` (p. 49).
To use this method, you can call it with the class name and the list of instance variables, like this:
This will generate the accessor methods for the instance variables `aaaa`, `bbbb`, and `cccc` in the class `MyClass`.

Alternatively, you can use the `DynamicAccessors` class, which automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand (p. 315). This class uses the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to trap messages not understood and dynamically load the missing methods on demand.

In Pharo, all instance variables are private to the object that owns them, so the only way for another object to access them is through accessor methods (p. 298). The `Inspector` uses the reflective abilities of Pharo to access instance variables without needing accessors (p. 298).
Best practices for generating getter/setter code automatically in Pharo or Squeak include:
* Using the `DynamicAccessors` class or a similar approach to generate accessor methods dynamically.
* Following the Pharo convention for naming getter and setter methods (p. 49).
* Keeping the code readable and maintainable by avoiding unnecessary complexity and following good coding practices (p. 36).

Note that arbitrary `Do its` are not recorded in a change set, so it's essential to use a systematic approach to generating getter/setter code (p. 153).",365,,,,,0.3333333333333333,0.6759496909772076,4,"['Let us look at a simple variant of this technique where we have a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand : DynamicAcccessors > > doesNotUnderstand :', 'In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .', '* Another typical use for overriding doesNotUnderstand : is to lazily load or compile missing methods .', 'In practice , the inspector can access instance variables without needing accessors , because it uses the reflective abilities of Pharo .']","['This class uses the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` method to trap messages not understood and dynamically load the missing methods on demand .', 'Alternatively , you can use the ` DynamicAccessors ` class , which automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand ( p. 315 ) .', 'In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to access them is through accessor methods ( p. 298 ) .', 'The ` Inspector ` uses the reflective abilities of Pharo to access instance variables without needing accessors ( p. 298 ) .']",373,1237,516,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,According to the textbook ""Pharo By Example"" (pp. 1, 100-101, 183-184), Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" because it does not have static type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. Instead, the type of an object is determined by its class, and the language uses dynamic message sends to determine the behavior of an object at runtime.

In Smalltalk, objects respond to messages (method calls) based on their class and the message sent. This means that the same message can be sent to different objects, each of which may have its own method for responding to the message (p. 100). The language does not provide static class operations
column2: instead, classes are simply objects that can receive messages (p. 100).

column1: The textbook also explains that Smalltalk's dynamic typing is different from being ""untyped"". While there are no explicit type annotations, the language still has a concept of type based on the class of an object. This is evident in the way objects respond to messages and in the use of class membership queries (p. 183-184).

In summary, Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because it uses dynamic message sends to determine the behavior of objects at runtime, without explicit type annotations. This approach allows for greater flexibility and polymorphism, but still maintains a concept of type based on the class of an object.",219,,,,,0.25,0.6640162760734969,2,"['For example , in100 6.8 Method lookup follows the inheritance chain stead of providing static class operations , we simply send messages to classes ( which are simply objects ) .', 'As a consequence , we can send the same message to different objects , each of which may have its own method for responding to the message .']","['The language does not provide static class operations
column2: instead , classes are simply objects that can receive messages ( p. 100 ) .', 'This means that the same message can be sent to different objects , each of which may have its own method for responding to the message ( p. 100 ) .']",224,1377,288,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a ""class variable"" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. ,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to your question:
In Pharo, ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"" are two distinct concepts that serve different purposes.

Instance variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that only the instance can access its own instance variables. This is in contrast to Java and C++, where instance variables (also known as fields or member variables) can be accessed by any other instance that happens to be of the same class. In Pharo, instance variables are similar to protected variables in C++ and Java, but it's considered bad style to access an instance variable directly from a subclass. Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses.

Class variables, on the other hand, are shared amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself. The lifetime of a class variable is the same as that of the class. Class variables are accessed through class methods, which are dynamically-dispatched methods, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends to work. Class variables are not to be confused with class instance variables, which are private to each class.
To illustrate the difference, consider an example:

In your `MyClass` definition, `instanceVariableNames: ''` defines the names of instance variables that are private to each instance of `MyClass`. On the other hand, `classVariableNames: ''` defines the names of class variables that are shared amongst all instances of `MyClass` and the class itself.
