RAG,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:

In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default because the language is designed to follow a uniform and simple programming model, where everything is an object, and objects communicate only by sending each other messages (p. 85). This means that when a method is invoked, it answers a value, which is an object, and if execution reaches the end of the method without executing a return statement (^), the method still answers a value – it answers the object that received the message, represented by the pseudo-variable ""self"" (p. 103).

This design choice is rooted in the philosophical background of Smalltalk, which emphasizes object-oriented programming, where objects are responsible for their own behavior and data. By returning ""self"" by default, methods can be chained together, allowing for more expressive and concise code (p. 103).

The idea behind this design is to encourage a style of programming where objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods that assume too much responsibility (p. 101). This is nicely summarized by Joseph Pelrine's principle: ""Don't do anything that you can push off onto someone else.""

In practical terms, this design choice has several benefits. For example, it allows for more flexible and dynamic method lookup, where methods can be overridden or extended by subclasses without affecting the original method (p. 104). It also encourages good object-oriented design principles, such as encapsulation and delegation, which lead to more modular, maintainable, and scalable code.

In summary, methods returning ""self"" by default in Smalltalk is a deliberate design choice that reflects the language's object-oriented programming philosophy and encourages good programming practices, such as method chaining, delegation, and encapsulation.",288,,,,,0.3333333333333333,0.6644280503001938,3,"[""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility ."", ', the method still answers a value - it answers the object that received the message .', 'Joseph Pelrine expresses this principle succinctly as follows : "" Do n\'t do anything that you can push off onto someone else . ""']","['This means that when a method is invoked , it answers a value , which is an object , and if execution reaches the end of the method without executing a return statement ( ^ ) , the method still answers a value - it answers the object that received the message , represented by the pseudo - variable "" self "" ( p. 103 ) .', 'The idea behind this design is to encourage a style of programming where objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility ( p. 101 ) .', 'This is nicely summarized by Joseph Pelrine \'s principle : "" Do n\'t do anything that you can push off onto someone else .""']",301,1303,372,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:

When a receiver doesn't understand a message, it sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the original message selector as its argument (p. 107, 312-313). This is a dynamic message send, and the lookup starts again from the class of the receiver, searching for a method corresponding to `doesNotUnderstand:`. If no such method is found, the lookup continues up the class hierarchy until it reaches `Object`, which implements `doesNotUnderstand:` (p. 107). This method creates a new `MessageNotUnderstood` object, capable of starting a Debugger in the current execution context (p. 107).

However, if the receiver is an instance of a subclass of `Object` that overrides `doesNotUnderstand:`, the object will respond differently to the `doesNotUnderstand:` message. This provides a convenient way to realize dynamic behavior, such as lazily loading or compiling missing methods (p. 313).

In your case, when you send a message to a receiver and it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error, it means that the receiver's class and its inheritance chain do not have a method corresponding to the message selector. To fix this, you can either add a method to the receiver's class or its superclass that responds to the message, or override `doesNotUnderstand:` in the receiver's class to provide an alternative behavior.

For example, if you have a class `EllipseMorph` and you want to respond to a message `#foo`, you can add a method `foo` to `EllipseMorph` or its superclass, or override `doesNotUnderstand:` in `EllipseMorph` to handle the `#foo` message.
Here's an example of how you can override `doesNotUnderstand:`:

Remember that `doesNotUnderstand:` cannot trap self-sends (p. 322), and some messages, like `class` and `yourself`, are never really sent, but are interpreted by the VM (p. 322).",304,,,,,0.7142857142857143,0.7733360707882285,5,"['An object that does not understand a message can , by overriding doesNotUnderstand : , fall back to an alternative strategy for responding to that message .', 'Now , this is a perfectly ordinary , dynamic message send , so the lookup starts again from the class EllipseMorph , but this time searching for the method doesNotUnderstand : .', 'But what if doesNotUnderstand : is overridden by one of the subclasses of Object in the lookup path ?', 'This method will create a new MessageNotUnderstood object which is capable of starting a Debugger in the current execution context .', 'The Pharo object model Listing 6 - 17 A locally implemented method EllipseMorph > >']","['an instance of a subclass of ` Object ` that overrides ` doesNotUnderstand : ` , the object will respond differently to the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` message', 'This is a dynamic message send , and the lookup starts again from the class of the receiver , searching for a method corresponding to ` doesNotUnderstand : ` .', 'If no such method is found , the lookup continues up the class hierarchy until it reaches ` Object ` , which implements ` doesNotUnderstand : ` ( p. 107 ) .', ""To fix this , you can either add a method to the receiver 's class or its superclass that responds to the message , or override ` doesNotUnderstand : ` in the receiver 's class to provide an alternative behavior ."", 'This method creates a new ` MessageNotUnderstood ` object , capable of starting a Debugger in the current execution context ( p. 107 ) .', ""Here 's an example of how you can override ` doesNotUnderstand : ` :   ` ` ` smalltalk   EllipseMorph > >""]",188,1404,478,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use the following approach (p. 214):
1. First, get the values of the dictionary using the `valuesDo:` message, which iterates over the values of the dictionary.
2. Then, convert the collection of values to a SortedCollection by sending the `asSortedCollection` message.
3. Finally, specify a sort block to define the sorting criterion, if necessary.
sortedRankables := a values asSortedCollection sortBlock: [ :v1 :v2 | v1 <= v2 ].

In this example, we first get the values of the dictionary using `valuesDo:`, which returns a collection of values. We then convert this collection to a SortedCollection using `asSortedCollection`. Finally, we specify a sort block that sorts the values in ascending order.
Note that if you want to sort the Rankable objects themselves, not just their values, you can modify the sort block accordingly. For example:
sortedRankables := a values asSortedCollection sortBlock: [ :r1 :r2 | r1 someMethod <= r2 someMethod ].
Here, `someMethod` should be replaced with the actual method that you want to use for sorting the Rankable objects.

Additionally, when working with arrays, be careful not to use mutable objects as elements, as this can lead to debugging issues (pp. 218-219).",175,,,,,0.0,0.5611967859651457,0,[],[],238,1358,263,0,[],pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

RAG+CoI,Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?,"

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
,As a Rubyist starting to explore Smalltalk, you're considering Squeak and Pharo as potential implementations to experiment with. Both options have their pros and cons.

Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language that supports live coding inspired by Smalltalk. It's highly portable, running on OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi. Pharo's virtual machine is written entirely in a subset of Pharo itself, making it easy to simulate, debug, analyze, and change from within Pharo itself.

Pharo strives to offer a lean, open platform for professional software development, as well as a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments. It serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework.
* Powerful IDE with tools for managing complex code and promoting good code design
* Rich library with a fast JITing VM and full access to OS libraries and features
* Culture that encourages and values changes and improvements
* May have a steeper learning curve due to its unique syntax and live coding environment
* Some users may find the Pharo community smaller compared to Squeak's
Squeak is a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80, and Pharo is actually a fork of Squeak. While Squeak is also a powerful platform, it may not be as actively maintained or developed as Pharo.
* Wide range of resources and documentation available
* May be more familiar to those with existing Smalltalk experience
* May not be as actively maintained or developed as Pharo

If you're looking for a more modern, highly portable, and actively maintained platform with a strong focus on live coding and a powerful IDE, Pharo might be the better choice. However, if you're already familiar with Smalltalk-80 and prefer a more established ecosystem with a wider range of resources, Squeak could be a good option.

Remember, both Pharo and Squeak are excellent choices for exploring Smalltalk, and you can't go wrong with either one.",326,,,,,0.4,0.6863318438923514,6,"['The Pharo project started in March 2008 as a fork of Squeak ( a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80 ) , and the first 1.0 beta version was released on July 31 , 2009 .', 'Pharo is a modern , open - Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

,Here are the answers to the questions:
**1. Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?**
Yes, it is possible to add a string to a String instance via a method. You can use the `append:` method or the `nextPutAll:` method on a WriteStream to concatenate strings.
**2. How can one define instance variables and methods in Pharo?**

In Pharo, instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. Instance variables are private to the instance itself, and accessor methods must be defined if they need to be accessed from outside the instance.
**3. What is a String in Pharo?**

In Pharo, a String is a collection of Characters. It is sequenceable, indexable, mutable, and homogeneous, containing only Character instances. Strings have a dedicated syntax and can be created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes.
**4. How can a string be modified using replaceAll:with:?**
A string can be modified using `replaceAll:with:` by replacing all occurrences of a specified substring with another substring.
**5. What is the difference in syntax for creating a String and a SortedCollection in Pharo?**
The syntax for creating a String is `'Hello'`, while the syntax for creating a SortedCollection is `SortedCollection new add: 5

,To check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it, you can use the `isKindOf:` method in Pharo. This method returns `true` if the receiver's class is the same as, or a subclass of, the argument class.
This method is useful for querying the class hierarchy and checking if an object is an instance of a specific class or one of its superclasses.
Additionally, you can use the `class` method to ask any object about its class, and the `isMemberOf:` method to check if an object is an instance of a specific class.

It's worth noting that Pharo has a reflective system, which allows you to navigate through its structure using the right combination of superclass and class messages (see Chapter: Classes and Metaclasses).
In summary, the methods that allow querying the class of an object in Pharo are:
* `class`: returns the class of an object
* `isMemberOf:`: checks if an object is an instance of a specific class
* `isKindOf:`: checks if an object is an instance of a class or one of its superclasses
* `respondsTo:`: checks if an object understands a specific message selector

These methods are useful for building development tools, debugging, and navigating the class hierarchy in Pharo.",219,,,,,0.3333333333333333,0.6481866511622085,2,"['Conversely , you can ask if an object is an instance of a specific class : 1 isMemberOf :', 'Here are a few other messages that might be useful to build development tools : isKindOf : aClass returns true if the receiver is instance of aClass or of one of its superclasses .']","['` class ` : returns the class of an object   * ` isMemberOf : ` : checks if an object is an instance of a specific class   * ` isKindOf : ` : checks if an object is an instance of a class or one of its superclasses   * ` respondsTo : ` : checks if an object understands a specific message selector    These methods are useful for building development tools , debugging , and navigating the class hierarchy in Pharo .', 'Additionally , you can use the ` class ` method to ask any object about its class , and the ` isMemberOf : ` method to check if an object is an instance of a specific class .']",187,4469,331,5,"['What methods allow querying the class of an object in Pharo?', 'What function does Object>>isKindOf perform?', 'What are instances of classes?', 'What kind of objects are classes?', 'What is ""Every object"" an instance of?']",pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:
To match all words in a phrase, you can use the regular expression `\w+` which matches one or more alphanumeric characters (equivalent to `[a-zA-Z0-9_]`).
For example, if you want to match all words in the phrase ""hello how are you"", you can use the following code:
'hello how are you' regex: '\w+' matchesCollect: [:word | word]
This will return an `OrderedCollection` containing all the words in the phrase: `('hello' 'how' 'are' 'you')`.
**What is the significance of 'word boundary' in the example regex?**

In the example regex, the word boundary `\b` is used to match an empty string at a word boundary. This means that the regex will match the start or end of a word, but not the middle. For example, the regex `.*\bw.*` will match a string that contains a word boundary before the character ""w"".
**Which characters require literal matching in regular expressions?**
In regular expressions, certain characters have special meanings. These characters are:
* `+` (match previous regex one or more times)
* `?` (match previous regex zero times or once)
* `|` (match choice of left and right regex)
To match these characters literally, you need to escape them with a backslash `\`.
**What does a set match in regex?**
In regex, a set (also known as a character class) matches any single character if it appears between the brackets. For example, `[01]` matches either 0 or 1.
`allRegexMatches` returns a collection of all matches (substrings of the receiver string) of the regular expression.

RAG+CoI,What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?,"

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
,Here is a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:
**What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?**
In Smalltalk, `self` and `super` are two important keywords that are often used within a method body.

`self` represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword `this` in Java (p. 103). It is a pseudo-variable that cannot be assigned to. When a method is executed, `self` always refers to the object that received the message (p. 60). In other words, `self` is the object inside which the current method is executing.

`super` also represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs (p. 105). This means that `super` is not the superclass of the class of the receiver, but rather it triggers a method lookup starting in the superclass of the class of the method performing the `super` send.

In summary, the main difference between `self` and `super` is the way the method lookup is performed. `self` lookup starts in the class of the receiver, while `super` lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

Additionally, `self` is often used to return the receiver of the message, especially when no explicit return value is specified (p. 103). This is because a method without an explicit return value will implicitly return `self`.
It's worth noting that there is no `yourself` keyword in Smalltalk, so the question is likely a mistake.

RAG+CoI,Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.
There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.
,Here is the detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:

In Pharo Smalltalk, you don't pass parameters to constructors like in Java and C++. Instead, you can use class variables, pool variables, or global variables to share data amongst instances and classes.

Class variables are shared between instances and classes. They are defined in the class definition and can be accessed by instances and the class itself. For example, `Color class >> initialize` initializes a class variable `ColorRegistry`.

Global variables are stored in a namespace called `Smalltalk` and are accessible everywhere. They are defined by assigning a value to a capitalized identifier, and the parser will offer to declare the global for you. For example, `Smalltalk globals at: #Boolean` answers `Boolean`.

To define a global, simply perform ""Do it"" on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier. The parser will then offer to declare the global for you. Alternatively, you can define a global programmatically by executing `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil`.
To create a global, you can do:
Then, you can access the global using `Smalltalk globals at: #MyGlobal`, which will answer `'Hello, World!'`.

It's recommended to limit the use of global variables and instead use class instance variables or class variables, and provide class methods to access them. This promotes encapsulation and avoids namespace pollution.",230,,,,,0.6363636363636364,0.7477694839359041,7,"['Class variables : variables shared between instances and classes .', 'If you want to define a global programmatically , just execute Smalltalk globals at : # AGlobalName put : nil .', 'Global variables In Pharo , all global variables are stored in a namespace called Smalltalk ,', 'Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them , and also in the methods defined in its subclasses .', 'The usual way to define a global is just to perform Do it on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier .', 'The parser will then offer to declare the global for you .', 'It is usually better to use class instance variables or class variables , and to provide class methods to access them .']","['Alternatively , you can define a global programmatically by executing ` Smalltalk globals at : # AGlobalName put : nil ` .', ""* * Example * *    To create a global , you can do :   ` ` `   Smalltalk globals at : # MyGlobal put : ' Hello , World ! RAG+CoI,For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk,"

I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter
It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.
2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'
For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)
The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.
4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error
<pre><code>  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:
the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.
5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random
The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
,Based on the provided text chunks, here are the answers to your questions:
**For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk**
In Pharo, you can use the `to:do:` method to create a loop that iterates over an array. For example:
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1].
This code creates an array of size 31 and populates it with random values between 0 and 1.

In Pharo, control constructs are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments. For example, conditional branches use the `ifTrue:` and `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages, while loops use the `whileTrue:` message.
**Methods to access and put elements in Pharo's arrays**
In Pharo, you can access and modify elements in an array using the `at:` and `at:put:` methods. For example:
anArray at: 1 put: 4 >>> sets element at index 1 to 4
**Most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk**
In Pharo, you can create instances of collections using the `new:` and `with:` methods. For example:
Array new: 5 >>> creates an array of size 5
Array with: 1 with: 2 with: 3 >>> creates an array with initial elements 1, 2, and 3
OrderedCollection withAll: #(4 5 6 1 2 3) >>> creates an ordered collection with all elements from the array
`OrderedCollection withAll:` is a message that creates an ordered collection with all elements from a given collection. For example:
(OrderedCollection withAll: #(4 5 6 1 2 3)) >>> creates an ordered collection with all elements from the array

RAG+CoI,Pharo method with multiple arguments,"

I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error ""Variable or expression expected..""
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :
<pre><code>    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,To answer the question, we need to combine relevant text chunks to provide a detailed and coherent explanation.
In Pharo, a method with multiple arguments is declared using a specific syntax. The method declaration syntax is as follows:
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
In this example, `MethodName` is the method name, and `arg1`, `arg2`, and `arg3` are the argument names. The `^` symbol is used to separate the method declaration from the method body.
To call this method, you would use the following syntax:
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

In Pharo, objects communicate with each other by sending messages. When sending a message, the sender does not decide which method will be executed. Instead, the receiver of the message decides how to respond by selecting its own method for doing what was asked. This is known as ""late-binding"" or ""dynamic method lookup"".

Pharo has three types of messages: unary, binary, and keyword messages. The order in which messages are sent is determined by the type of message. Unary messages are sent first, then binary messages, and finally keyword messages. Parentheses can be used to change the order of execution.
In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, and class variables are shared amongst all instances of a class and the class itself.

Method lookup follows the inheritance chain. When sending a message, the receiver decides how to respond by selecting its own method for doing what was asked. The method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, and if not found, it continues up the inheritance chain.

Pharo's syntax is minimal and designed to be read aloud like a kind of pidgin English. Expressions are built up from a very small number of primitive elements, such as message sends, assignments, closures, and returns. There are only 6 keywords, and there is no syntax for control structures or declaring new classes. Instead, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects.",340,,,,,0.7222222222222222,0.8030478584176374,13,"['Pharo distinguishes such three types of messages to minimize the number of RAG+CoI,SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:
When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.
Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

,The issue here is that `Transcript` is not a receiver that understands the `show:` message. `Transcript` is a kind of system console, often used for logging system messages. To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` object using `Transcript`, you need to send the `show:` message to the `Transcript` object, passing the collection's string representation as an argument.
This will print the contents of the `OrderedCollection` object to the `Transcript` console.

In Pharo, `OrderedCollection` is a class that can grow and to which elements can be added sequentially. It offers a variety of messages such as `add:`, `addFirst:`, `addLast:`, and `addAll:` (pages 204-205). You can also use messages like `asString` to convert the collection to a string representation.

The `Transcript` object is used for logging system messages and can be used to print any object's string representation using the `show:` message (page 16).",156,,,,,0.375,0.6673081095225576,3,"['The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages .', '217 Basic classes The following code yields the expected result : | collection | collection : = OrderedCollection new .', 'OrderedCollection OrderedCollection is one of the collections that can grow , and to which elements can be added sequentially .']","['` Transcript ` is a kind of system console , often used for logging system messages .', ""The ` Transcript ` object is used for logging system messages and can be used to print any object 's string representation using the ` show : ` message ( page 16 ) ."", ""Here 's the correct code :   ` ` `   | oc |   oc : = OrderedCollection new ."", 'In Pharo , ` OrderedCollection ` is a class that can grow and to which elements can be added sequentially .']",216,4161,253,5,"['What message is sent when the result of (OrderedCollection withAll: #(4 5 6 1 2 3)) asSortedCollection is given to a class?', 'What is ""OrderedCollection withAll"" in Pharo?', 'How can elements be added to OrderedCollection?', 'What messages are being sent to ""OrderedCollection"" and ""asSortedCollection""?', 'What is OrderedCollection a superclass of?']",pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

,Here is a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:
**How do we write if elseif elseif in Pharo?**
In Pharo, control constructs are not expressed using special syntax. Instead, they are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments. (p. 66)
Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to the result of a boolean expression. (p. 66)

Loops are typically expressed by sending messages to blocks, integers, or collections. Since the exit condition for a loop may be repeatedly evaluated, it should be a block rather than a boolean value. (p. 66)

The conditional branches of `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` require blocks, which are created using square brackets `[]`. This is because we do not know how many times the expression should be executed. (p. 83)

In summary, to write `if elseif elseif` in Pharo, you would use a combination of `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` messages with blocks as arguments, and use square brackets `[]` to create block Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows
Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
<li>It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
• when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:
yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
,The question is asking why the implementation of `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` should be `self subclassResponsibility` instead of `^self size`.

The answer lies in the concept of abstract methods and abstract classes in Pharo. An abstract class is a class that exists to be subclassed, rather than to be instantiated. An abstract class is usually incomplete, in the sense that it does not define all of the methods that it uses. The ""placeholder"" methods, those that the other methods assume to be (re)defined, are called abstract methods.

In Pharo, abstract methods are implemented by convention with the body `self subclassResponsibility`. This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method. `self subclassResponsibility` methods should always be overridden, and thus should never be executed. If you forget to override one, and it is executed, an exception will be raised.

By implementing `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` as `self subclassResponsibility`, you are signaling to subclasses that they must override this method with their own implementation. This is a way to ensure that subclasses provide their own implementation of the `size` method, rather than relying on a default implementation that may not be suitable.

In contrast, implementing `size` as `^self size` would simply return the receiver of the message, which is not what you want in this case. You want to force subclasses to provide their own implementation of the `size` method, which is what `self subclassResponsibility` achieves.",233,,,,,0.7,0.8517352600770065,7,"['Abstract methods are Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:
To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword ""new"" to create a new instance as under:
When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-
However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as :
1.  What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error?
2.  The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods
3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?
Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.

,

I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:
""and here you probably have something like:""

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? ""createUsers""? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as ""users"", and then the responsibility of the class object is ""to create users (its instances)"".
Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:
instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
or a shorter version using cascaded messages:
initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the ""class class"". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:
This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.
3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
,Here are the answers to the questions:
**1. Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0**

* In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself and can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. (p. 89)
* Class instance variables are similar to protected variables in C++ and Java, but are considered private in Pharo. (p. 89)
* Class variables are shared between instances and classes, and are not to be confused with class instance variables. (p. 92)
* Class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, which means that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods in Pharo, unlike static methods in Java. (p. 92)
**2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods**
* Pharo's class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, unlike Java's static methods, which are statically-resolved procedures. (p. 92)
* In Pharo, class variables are shared between instances and classes, whereas in Java, static variables are shared between all instances of a class. (p. 92)
* In Pharo, instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. (p. 89)
* Accessor methods must be defined if instance variables need to be accessed from outside the class. (p. 89)

* Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself and can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. (p. 89)
**What is the distinction between instance variables in Pharo and in Java/C++?**

* In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, whereas in Java and C++, instance variables (also known as fields or member variables) can be accessed by any other instance that happens to be of the same class. (p. 89)
* Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk. (p. 1)
* Pharo is highly portable and can run on various platforms, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi. (p. 2)
**What are class variables in the Pharo object model?**
* Class variables are shared between instances and classes, and are not to be confused with class instance variables. (p. 92)
* Class variables are variables shared between a class, its subclasses, and its instances. (p. 109)
* Pharo's syntax is minimal, with only six reserved identifiers (also called pseudo-variables): true, false, nil, self, super, and thisContext. (p. 69)

* Pharo's syntax is designed so that program text can be read aloud as though it were a kind of pidgin English. ,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or

,Here are the answers to the questions:
**How can I break out of an infinite loop in Pharo?**

If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of the loop by typing `CMD-.` (that's a full stop or a period, depending on where you learned English). This will interrupt the execution and open a debugger window. You can also insert `Halt now` into the suspect code to bring up the pre-debugger.
**How can one set a breakpoint in Pharo without using `self halt`?**
You can set a breakpoint in Pharo by using `Halt once` or `Halt if: aCondition`. You can also use `Object>>haltIf:` to conditionally halt only if you are in a context of interest.
**What are a few other ways to get into the debugger in the Pharo environment?**

You can get into the debugger by running a failing test, by typing `CMD-.` to interrupt a running process, or by inserting a `Halt now` expression in code. You can also open the debugger by clicking on any of the lines on the stack trace.",173,,,,,0.875,0.810702299053142,7,"['There are a few other ways to get into the debugger in addition to catching 139 Some of the key tools of the Pharo environment Figure 7 - 24 Changing the suffix method in the debugger : fixing the off - by - one error after an SUnit assertion failure .', 'You can also just edit the suspect code to insert Halt now .', 'Intelligent breakpoints The Pharo way to set a breakpoint is to evaluate self halt at an interesting point in a method .', 'You can also open the debugger by clicking on any of the lines on the stack trace .', 'This is exactly what Object>>haltIf : offers .', ""( that 's a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) ."", 'When this message is sent , execution will be interrupted and a debugger will open to this point in your program ( see Chapter : The Pharo Environment for more details about the debugger ) .']","['* * What are a few other ways to get into the debugger in the Pharo environment ? **Creating a new class in Pharo Smalltalk:**
To create a new class in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to:
1. **Create a new package**: Right-click in the package pane and select ""Add package..."".

2. **Create a new class**: In the browser, click the currently selected package (in the left-most pane), and the bottom browser pane will display a class creation template. Edit this template to replace ""Object"" with the name of the existing class of which you wish to create a subclass, replace ""NameOfSubclass"" with the name that you would like to give to your new subclass, and fill in the instance variable names if you know them.
3. **Write Smalltalk code**: You need to write some Smalltalk code to define the new class.
4. **Accept the changes**: Either right-click and select ""Accept"", or use the shortcut CMD-s (for ""Save"").
5. **The class is created**: The message will be sent to the superclass, which will cause the new class to be compiled. The class is now created and appears in the class pane of the browser.

,To generate getter/setter code automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can create a method that takes a list of instance variables as an argument and dynamically generates the accessor methods.
Here is an example of how you can achieve this:
The `compileAllAccessorsFor:` method can be implemented as follows:
self class compile: variable, String cr, ' ^ ', variable.
self class compile: variable, ':', String cr, ' ^ ', variable ]
This will generate getter and setter methods for each instance variable in the list.
Alternatively, you can use the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to dynamically generate accessor methods on demand. Here is an example:
self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
This will generate the accessor methods dynamically when they are first accessed.

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:

Smalltalk (and Pharo) is called ""dynamically typed"" because it does not have explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. Instead, the type of an object is determined by the class of the object, which is resolved at runtime. This means that the type system is dynamic, and the type of an object can change during the execution of the program.

In Pharo, objects respond to messages by sending messages to themselves or other objects. This message-passing mechanism allows objects to dynamically determine their behavior based on the messages they receive. The type of an object is not fixed at compile-time, but rather it is determined by the class of the object, which can be changed at runtime.

Additionally, Pharo's syntax is designed to minimize the need for explicit type annotations. The language relies heavily on message passing, and objects respond to messages based on their class and the methods they provide. This approach allows for more flexibility and dynamic behavior, which is a key characteristic of dynamically typed languages.

In contrast, statically typed languages, such as Java, require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. The type system is resolved at compile-time, and the type of an object is fixed and cannot be changed at runtime.

So, to summarize, Smalltalk (and Pharo) is called ""dynamically typed"" because it does not have explicit type annotations, and the type of an object is determined by the class of the object, which is resolved at runtime. This allows for more flexibility and dynamic behavior, which is a key characteristic of dynamically typed languages.",278,,,,,0.2307692307692307,0.5893378449613791,3,"['Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', 'Everything is an object , and pretty much everything happens by sending messages .', ""Pharo 's syntax is minimal .""]","['In Pharo , objects respond to messages by sending messages to themselves or other objects .', 'The language relies heavily on message passing , and objects respond to messages based on their class and the methods they provide .', ""Additionally , Pharo 's syntax is designed to minimize the need for explicit type annotations .""]",224,6031,357,5,"['Why is Pharo a reflective system?', 'What is one of the unique features of Pharo (and its ancestors)?', 'Why is Pharo based on sending messages to objects?', 'Which programming language is Pharo based on?', 'Why is the syntax of Pharo so simple?']",pharo,llama3:70b-instruct

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.
Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. ,Here is a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to your question:
**What is the difference between ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"", in Pharo?**
In Pharo, ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"" serve different purposes.

Instance variables are private to the instance itself. This means that two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object. (p. 89)

Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. This means that Pharo instance variables are similar to protected variables in C++ and Java. However, it is considered bad style in Pharo to access an instance variable directly from a subclass.

Class variables are shared between instances and classes. The term class variable indicates that the lifetime of the variable is the same as that of the class. However, what the term does not convey is that these variables are shared amongst all the instances of a class as well as the class itself. (pp. 109 - 110)

Pharo provides three kinds of shared variables: globally shared variables, class variables, and pool variables. The names of all of these shared variables start with a capital letter, to warn us that they are indeed shared between multiple objects.

In summary, instance variables are private to each instance, while class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself.",264,,,,,1.0,0.8682586166226707,12,"['The term class variable indicates that the lifetime of the variable is the same as that of the class .', 'This means that Pharo instance variables are similar to protected variables in C++ and Java .', ""In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods ."", 'Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', 'Class variables : variables shared between instances and classes .', 'Class variables are private variables shared by the class and all the instances of the class .', 'Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them , and also in the methods defined in its subclasses .', 'However , we prefer to say that they are private , because it is considered bad style in Pharo to access an instance variable directly from a subclass .', 'There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object .', 'Pharo provides three kinds of shared variables : 1 .', 'The capitalization of these variables gives us a hint that they are shared .', 'However , what the term does not convey is that these variables are shared amongst all the instances of a class as well as the class itself , as shown in Figure 6 - RAG+CoI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,Here is a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:
**Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?**

In Smalltalk, if a method does not explicitly return a value, it implicitly returns the receiver of the message, which is represented by the pseudo-variable `self`. This is because in Pharo, everything is an object, and objects communicate only by sending each other messages. Methods are all public and dynamically looked up (late-bound).

When a method does not explicitly return a value, it is equivalent to returning `self` at the end of the method. This means that the method answers the object that received the message, which is the receiver of the message. This is different from languages like Ruby, where methods return the value of the last statement in the method by default.

The philosophical background behind this design is to encourage a style of programming where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods that assume too much responsibility. This is in line with the principles of object-oriented programming.

One of the practical benefits of this design is that it facilitates method chaining, where an object can send a series of messages to itself or other objects, without having to explicitly return a value at each step. This makes the code more concise and easier to read.
Additionally, this design allows for more flexibility and dynamic behavior, as methods can be overridden or redefined in subclasses, and the method lookup follows the inheritance chain.

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?

,

Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.
If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:
<pre><code>(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:
This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
,To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` method. Here's an example:
data := received data after each refresh.
""How to check if this data already exist and not add""

This code creates a new set `z` and then iterates over the `data` collection. For each element `list` in `data`, it checks if the element is already in the set `z` using the `includes:` method. If the element is not in the set, it adds the element to the set using the `add:` method.

Note that `includes:` is a method that returns a boolean value indicating whether the element is in the set or not. If the element is not in the set, `ifFalse:` is used to execute the block that adds the element to the set.
Alternatively, you can use the `add:` method with a block that checks if the element is already in the set, like this:
data := received data after each refresh.
z add: list ifAbsent: [z add: list]

This code uses the `add:` method with a block that checks if the element is already in the set using the `ifAbsent:` method. ,Here is the detailed answer to the question:
**How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk**

In Pharo Smalltalk, a switch is not implemented using a traditional switch statement like in other programming languages. Instead, conditionals are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments.
For example, to implement a switch, you can use the `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` messages, like this:

This code sends the `ifTrue:` message to the result of the expression `17 * 13 > 220`, which is a boolean value. If the result is true, the block `[ 'bigger' ]` is executed, otherwise the block `[ 'smaller' ]` is executed.
**How are control constructs expressed in Pharo?**

In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to objects, rather than using traditional control structures like if-then-else statements or loops. For example, conditionals are expressed by sending the `ifTrue:` or `ifFalse:` messages to a boolean object, and loops are expressed by sending messages to blocks, integers, or collections.
**How do objects respond to a message in Pharo?**

In Pharo, when an object receives a message, it first looks up the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message. If no such method exists, it will continue looking up the class hierarchy, until it reaches Object. If still no method is found for that message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message, which will launch the debugger.
**How are the conditional branches of ifTrue: and ifFalse: in Smalltalk implemented?**

The conditional branches of `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` are implemented using blocks as arguments. The `ifTrue:` method takes two blocks as arguments, and executes the first block if the receiver is true, and the second block if the receiver is false. The `ifFalse:` method is implemented similarly.

RAG+CoI,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
,Here is a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to the question:

When a receiver does not understand a message, it sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument (p. 107, p. 312). This is a perfectly ordinary, dynamic message send, and the lookup starts again from the class of the receiver, searching for the `doesNotUnderstand:` method.
**What method does an object call when it cannot find a method for the message it received?**
When an object cannot find a method for the message it received, it calls the `doesNotUnderstand:` method (p. 107, p. 312).
**What is created when the Object's `doesNotUnderstand:` method is invoked?**
When the Object's `doesNotUnderstand:` method is invoked, a new `MessageNotUnderstood` object is created, which is capable of starting a Debugger in the current execution context (p. 107).
**Why does the virtual machine make an object send `doesNotUnderstand:` when a method is not found?**
The virtual machine makes an object send `doesNotUnderstand:` when a method is not found because this offers developers an easy way to intercept such errors and take alternative action (p. 107).

In Pharo, messages are understood by sending a message to an object, and the object, not the sender, selects the appropriate method for responding to the message (p. 14). The method associated with the message is looked up dynamically, and the lookup process follows the inheritance chain (p. 101).
**What happens when a method does not explicitly return a value?**
When a method does not explicitly return a value, it returns `nil` by default (p. 101).

,The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is a common idiom in Python that allows a script to be executed as a standalone program or imported as a module without executing the script.

When a Python script is executed directly (e.g., by running `python script.py`), the special variable `__name__` is set to the string `""__main__""`. This indicates that the script is being executed as the main program.
When a Python script is imported as a module (e.g., by running `import script`), the `__name__` variable is set to the name of the module (e.g., `""script""`).

The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement checks whether the script is being executed directly or imported as a module. If it's being executed directly, the code inside the `if` block is executed. If it's being imported as a module, the code inside the `if` block is skipped.
When you run this script directly (e.g., `python script.py`), the `__name__` variable is set to `""__main__""`, and the `print` statement is executed, printing ""Hello, World!"" to the console.
However, if you import this script as a module (e.g., `import script`), the `__name__` variable is set to `""script""`, and the `print` statement is skipped.
Why should you include this `if` statement?

1. **Prevent unintended execution**: When you import a script as a module, you might not want it to execute the entire script. The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement ensures that only the intended code is executed when the script is imported.

2. **Allow for testing and debugging**: By including this statement, you can write test cases or debugging code that only runs when the script is executed directly, without affecting the behavior of the script when imported as a module.
3. **Enable modular programming**: This idiom allows you to write scripts that can be used as both standalone programs and modules, promoting modular programming and code reuse.

In summary, the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is a guard clause that ensures a script is only executed when run directly, and not when imported as a module. It's a fundamental concept in Python programming that promotes",349,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.5973600454139314,2,"['Otherwise , if the module is being imported , the test code is skipped .', ""If the program is running as a script , _ _ name _ _ has the value ' _ _""]","[""If it 's being imported as a module , the code inside the ` if ` block is skipped ."", 'When a Python script is imported as a module ( e.g. , by running ` import script ` ) , the ` _ _ name _ _']",260,1330,516,0,[],python,llama3:70b-instruct

Python calls this concept ""slicing"" and it works on more than just strings. Take a look here for a comprehensive introduction.
,In Python, you can get a substring of a string using slicing. The syntax for slicing is `string[start:stop:step]`, where:
* `start` is the index of the first character you want to include in the substring (inclusive).
* `stop` is the index of the last character you want to include in the substring (exclusive).
* `step` is the increment between characters (default is 1).
To get a substring from the third character to the end of the string, you can use `myString[2:]`.
Here's a breakdown of how slicing works:
* If you omit the `stop` part, it means ""to the end"" of the string. For example, `myString[2:]` gets all characters from the third character to the end of the string.
* If you omit the `start` part, it means ""from the start"" of the string. For example, `myString[:5]` gets all characters from the start of the string to the fifth character (exclusive).
* If you omit both `start` and `stop`, it returns a copy of the original string. For example, `myString[:]` returns the entire string.

,In Python, the `yield` keyword is used to define a generator function. A generator function is a special type of function that returns an iterator, which allows it to produce a sequence of values over time, rather than computing them all at once and returning them in a list, for example.

When a generator function is called, it doesn't execute immediately. Instead, it returns a generator iterator, which is an object that can be iterated over to retrieve the values produced by the generator function.

In the example code, the `_get_child_candidates` method is a generator function because it uses the `yield` keyword. When this method is called, it returns a generator iterator. The `yield` statements in the method are used to produce values, but they don't return a list or a single element. Instead, they produce values one at a time, and the execution of the method is suspended until the next value is requested.
Here's what happens when the `_get_child_candidates` method is called:
1. The method is called, and it returns a generator iterator.
2. The execution of the method is suspended at the first `yield` statement.
3. The caller can iterate over the generator iterator to retrieve the values produced by the method. In this case, the caller uses the `extend` method to add the values to the `candidates` list.
4. When the caller iterates over the generator iterator, the execution of the method resumes from where it was suspended.
5. The method executes until it reaches the next `yield` statement, at which point it suspends execution again and produces the next value.

6. This process continues until the method reaches the end of its execution, at which point the generator iterator is exhausted, and the `candidates` list is populated with all the values produced by the method.

In this specific example, the `_get_child_candidates` method is called repeatedly to retrieve child candidates, and the `candidates` list is populated with these candidates. The method is called again because the `candidates` list is extended with the values produced by the method, which includes the child candidates. The subsequent calls will stop when the `candidates` list is empty, indicating that all child candidates have been retrieved.

When we call the function, it returns a generator iterator, which we can iterate over using the `next` function. Each time we call `next`, the execution of the method resumes from where it was suspended, and the next value is produced. ,To execute a program or call a system command in Python, you can use the `os` module, specifically the `popen` function, which represents a running program (p. 142). This allows you to launch any program that you can launch from the shell, such as changing directories with `cd`, displaying the contents of a directory with `ls`, or launching a web browser by typing (for example) `firefox`.
For example, you can use `os.popen` to execute the Unix command `ls -l`, which normally displays the contents of the current directory in long format:

The `popen` function returns an object that behaves like an open file, allowing you to read the output from the `ls` process one line at a time with `readline` or get the whole thing at once with `read`:
When you are done, you close the pipe like a file:
The return value is the final status of the `ls` process

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.

• Your module is a library, but you want to have a script mode where it runs some unit tests or a demo.

• Your module is mostly used as a main program, but it also provides a programmer-friendly API for advanced users.
